title: Wasteland Encounters
description: The broken world between settlements — irradiated flats, collapsed highways, toxic waterways, and the long silence of a civilization that didn't make it. What moves through the wasteland is either surviving, scavenging, or hunting.

entries.result: A traveler in homemade radiation gear who waves the party down to trade water for information about what's ahead.
The collapsed remains of a highway overpass that still has pre-collapse graffiti readable on the underside, including one message that was left recently.
A pack of feral dogs keeping distance, following at the edge of visibility. They've been following for two hours.
A crater, wide and shallow, filled with standing water that is the wrong color. Something is floating in it that isn't debris.
A row of pre-collapse vehicles on a road, keys still in some of them, as if everyone left in a hurry and never came back — because they didn't.
A working wind turbine, small and hand-built, feeding power to something underground. No one is around.
A raider scout watching the party from a distance who isn't engaging — just watching, counting, reporting.
A stretch of ground where nothing grows. Not desert — just a circle where things tried to grow and stopped. Precise edges.
A hand-painted sign pointing toward a settlement the party has heard of but that was reportedly abandoned years ago.
A caravan of survivors moving in the opposite direction, clearly fleeing something, who will answer one question before they keep moving.
A pre-collapse bunker entrance, hatch open, ventilation still running. The last person who opened it isn't near it anymore.
A child, alone, who knows this area better than anyone should and won't say how they got here.
An intact vending machine — still powered, somehow — in the middle of nowhere, dispensing something that isn't what the label says.
A gang's territorial marker: a vehicle chassis on a pole, painted with a symbol. The symbol has been crossed out and repainted twice.
The sound of machinery somewhere below the ground — rhythmic, mechanical, ongoing — with no visible surface structure.
A solar panel array, mostly intact, with wires leading to a building that burned down years ago. Someone has recently reconnected the wires to something else.
Three bodies arranged in a circle, equidistant from each other, no visible cause of death, no salvage taken.
A merchant with a mule cart who has no apparent fear of the open wasteland and prices accordingly.
A water source marked on older maps that has run dry. Someone has scratched off the mark and written an arrow.
A stretch of road where every vehicle that attempted it is still there, stopped, in various states of collapse. The road looks passable.
A pre-collapse billboard still legible, advertising something that used to seem normal and now reads as a document from another species.
A pit trap — crude, effective — dug into a trail with a small amount of effort and a complete absence of malice toward any specific target.
A settlement watchtower, abandoned, with a logbook inside. The last entries are dated two years ago. They stop mid-sentence.
A radiation zone with a clear visible boundary — dead vegetation on one side, living on the other — and a path through it that someone has marked with flags.
A pre-collapse gas station still structurally sound, used as a shelter, with a message board of notes from travelers passing through.
A drone, damaged, running at reduced capacity, that has been tracking the party for about an hour and belongs to someone.
A field of pre-collapse solar panels, most broken, a few still working. Someone has been harvesting the working ones systematically. They're halfway done.
A group of scavengers who have found something large and are in the process of arguing about how to move it.
A dead zone where communications equipment goes silent — radios, GPS, everything — for about a kilometer, then resumes normally.
An armed escort moving a water tanker, watching the road ahead and behind, not interested in conversation, not hostile unless given a reason.
A crater from a pre-collapse military strike with something in the center that wasn't there when the strike happened.
A cult encampment, peaceful, organized, welcoming — which is either genuine community or a warning sign depending on experience level.
The remains of a pre-collapse emergency response vehicle, stripped of almost everything, with one intact cabinet that requires a key that's somewhere inside.
A stretch of highway where someone has been maintaining the road surface — filling potholes, clearing debris — without any settlement nearby.
A lone sniper position, recently vacated, with spent casings and enough equipment left behind to suggest the departure was unplanned.
A pre-collapse children's playground, almost entirely intact, that the local wildlife has adopted as territory.
A settlement trader operating from a fortified vehicle who will trade for almost anything and asks too many questions about where the party came from.
A field of land mines — old, unstable — with a safe path through that someone has marked by placing stones. The stones look deliberate.
A crashed aircraft, pre-collapse, that still has cargo in the hold. The hold requires a code to open and the code is written on the underside of the pilot's seat.
A temporary camp of wasteland wanderers who have stopped here because one of their group is sick and they're not moving until she can walk.
A pre-collapse communications tower still broadcasting a signal — not emergency band, not a beacon, something else.
A group of children playing in the wreckage of a pre-collapse suburb, supervised by no visible adults, who know exactly where the nearest settlement is.
A section of wasteland that the local survivor network calls bad ground. Nobody explains why. Everyone agrees about the boundary.
A pair of scavengers who've found a locked pre-collapse safe and have been at it for six hours with incorrect tools.
A pre-collapse water treatment facility, mostly destroyed, with one functional tank still holding clean water. Someone has found this already.
A raider camp, temporarily abandoned, with a map on the wall showing current settlement locations — including some the party didn't know about.
A river crossing where the bridge is down and someone has been charging for ferry service using a raft they built themselves. The price is negotiable.
A section of ground that has been cultivated — recently, carefully — where nothing should be able to grow. The cultivator isn't here.
A pre-collapse pharmacy, structurally sound, stripped of almost everything medical. One cabinet behind a broken wall has been missed.
An automated system — irrigation, pest control, something — still running on its pre-collapse programming, tending a field that no longer exists for people who are no longer here.
A survivor who has been living alone in the wasteland for years and has developed a very specific, very functional approach to it that doesn't map to any settlement's norms.
A section of road blocked by a vehicle that's been pushed across it deliberately. Whoever pushed it is not here. Whatever they were blocking is behind the vehicle.
A pre-collapse hospital, collapsed on one side, accessible on the other, with the pharmacy and records room in the collapsed section.
A dust storm approaching — visible, coming fast — with shelter available if the party moves in the next ten minutes and questionable if they don't.
A military convoy — settlement militia or faction force — moving in force along a route that suggests they're heading toward something specific.
A working radio on a pre-collapse frequency broadcasting a loop that sounds human-recorded but may be automated. The content is directions.
A scavenger who has just found something significant and hasn't decided yet whether to tell anyone. She's still at the site, still deciding.
A pre-collapse suburb where one house has lights on, smoke from the chimney, and a truck out front. Everyone else left years ago.
A series of markers — improvised cairns, painted rocks — leading somewhere, placed by someone with enough time and care to suggest they expected others to follow.
A dead raider group — all of them — in a position that suggests they were moving and stopped simultaneously. No evidence of conflict.
A pre-collapse research facility's satellite dish, intact, that someone has been attempting to power up using a generator that keeps failing.
An ambush position set up for the party's route that has been abandoned — gear still in place — sometime in the last hour.
A faction outpost at a crossroads, manned, enforcing a toll that is technically a fee for 'route maintenance' they're not doing.
A pre-collapse school with a basement shelter that was used during the collapse and still has supplies — and evidence of what happened to the people who used them.
A massive pre-collapse piece of equipment — mining, construction, industrial — abandoned mid-operation, still in perfect mechanical condition.
A person who insists the collapse didn't happen the way everyone says it did, has documentation to support an alternative account, and is difficult to dismiss.
A pre-collapse grocery store being used as a base of operations by a group that has figured out the cold storage still partially works.
A field of solar distillers — improvised, effective — producing clean water with no one attending them. Someone built these and left.
A message scratched into a concrete barrier in multiple languages, left by someone who wanted everyone passing to be able to read it.
A pack animal — horse, mule, something — without a rider, still saddled, stopped at the roadside, not distressed, waiting.
A survivor settlement too small to be on any map, populated by people who chose not to be on any map.
A pre-collapse data center, mostly destroyed, with one server rack still powered by a solar hookup. Someone has been trying to access it.
A section of landscape that looks pre-collapse from a distance and is, on approach, a very thorough illusion.
A trade caravan with professional security and cargo they won't describe, on a route that doesn't go between any settlements the party knows.
A pre-collapse national park sign, mostly legible, at the entrance to a wilderness area that has been reclaimed completely by something that isn't the original wildlife.
A working hand pump at a pre-collapse rest stop, delivering water from a depth that suggests someone has maintained the pump recently.
A group of settlers attempting to establish a new homestead in a location that experienced survivors would not choose. They've been here two months.
A pre-collapse bridge still intact, gated, with a small settlement on the far side that controls access and charges for crossing.
A dead wasteland creature that's too large to have been killed by anything the party has seen in this region.
A preacher of the old world standing at a crossroads, not threatening, not demanding, just insisting that people deserve to know what happened and why.
A pre-collapse military base perimeter, the fence still largely intact, with signs of recent entry that the base's automated systems have not responded to.
A survivor carrying pre-collapse tech they don't understand but have kept functional through observation and intuition.
A faction enforcer operating alone outside their territory, doing something their faction would not officially sanction.
A freshwater spring that locals know about and protect, marked on no map, accessible only to people who know the landmarks.
A pre-collapse weather monitoring station still operational, broadcasting data to no receiver, that is currently detecting something unusual.
A dead drop — pre-collapse spy infrastructure — that someone has found and repurposed for current post-collapse communication.
A long-range scout from a settlement the party knows, moving through wasteland they have no obvious mission in, who provides a plausible cover story.
A section of wasteland with no debris at all — no collapsed structures, no wreckage, no bodies — as if it was cleared deliberately after the collapse.
A survivor who has mapped the wasteland in this region in exhaustive detail and will trade the map for something specific.
A pre-collapse automated security system — turrets, sensors — still operational, still enforcing a perimeter around something long since gone.
A group of idealists attempting to restore a pre-collapse institution — school, clinic, court — with the resources available to them, which are insufficient.
A scavenger who has been poisoned by something they found and knows they have a specific amount of time. They have information and want to trade it for help.
A pre-collapse emergency broadcast system tower sending a continuous signal. The signal has been modified from its original content. Someone is still adding to it.
A territorial dispute between two factions visible in the landscape — alternating markers, painted-over symbols, and two observation posts watching each other across a dead zone.
A survivor community built inside a pre-collapse structure that's too large for their current population, with whole sections sealed off that they haven't opened.
A working vehicle in good condition parked on the open road with a full tank, no occupants, no damage, and no obvious reason it was left.
A settlement's supply runner, dead on the trail, with the supplies intact. Whatever killed them didn't want the supplies.
A section of wasteland where the sky looks different — color, light quality, cloud formation — from a specific angle, in a way that has no meteorological explanation.
A pre-collapse bunker complex entrance, sealed from inside, with a message scratched on the exterior that reads: do not open. We are still in here. We are fine. Do not open.
The wasteland is quiet tonight in a way that experienced travelers know. Every living thing has gone to ground. Something is moving through the dark that has learned not to make noise.
